task: Write a Python function merge_within(pairs) taking a list of [start, end] inclusive integer ranges, possibly unsorted, and returning the merged ranges sorted by start, as a list of [start, end] lists. Ranges that overlap or share an endpoint merge: [1, 4] and [3, 6] become [1, 6], and [1, 3] and [3, 5] become [1, 5]. Ranges separated by any gap stay separate. An empty list returns []. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: Function appears to correctly merge the input ranges, but the naming of edge cases could be improved.
trace: I used a simple sorting approach on the input list to ensure that the ranges are sorted by their starting points. I then created an empty list for the merged ranges and appended the first range from the input list to it. Afterwards, I iterated through the remaining ranges in the input list. If the ending point of the last merged range was greater than or equal to the starting point of the current range, I merged them by updating the end point of the last merged range with the maximum value between its original end and the end of the current range. Otherwise, I appended the current range to the merged list. This approach handles the edge case where there are no input ranges (an empty list).
